en: Matthew, last time I saw you, we were in U.T. Austin. Yes, sir.
Where you were a professor at that university. I still am a professor.
You still are. Yeah, that's right. I kept my job. How was the class? How did it go?
Went great. Everyone passed. Everyone got A's. Yeah, at first.
We actually studied this film, The Gentleman. You did?
Oh, that's right. And I saw from your Lincoln ads
that over the holiday, you went ice fishing in the back of your truck.
Everyone's always known how big of an ice fisherman I've been.
Yeah. Had you ever, ever been ice fishing? Absolutely not.
But I play one on TV. Let the red flag come up and get a bit of a smile.
Go get the fish very slowly. Have you seen his Lincoln ads?
No, I want to very much. Oh, they're the most relaxing, coolest, best.
One of the oddest things you'll ever see. Really?
They're fantastic. I'd never know where he's going. He's quite an odd man, actually.
You think he is? Yeah. Did you guys ever know each other before this movie?
No, and not on it, either. We had no scenes together.
So we've really just met. Just now. Dude.
Wow. We passed each other from a distance
on a Chinese golf course, someone told us 15 years ago.
Is that right? But now we're just meeting tonight, yeah.
There's a question I wanted to ask you because I think it was last year you got a little political in England.
You didn't want to get Boris Johnson re-elected.
So you went, is this true, you went door-to-door knocking on people's doors, telling them not to vote for Boris Johnson?
I did. I did.
What did people do? What? Well, they pretended to be very nice.
I took eight different candidates around and, you know, we knocked on doors
and I said, are you going to vote for Jim? And they all said, oh, yes, very much so.
And none of them did. It didn't work.
Every single one of my candidates lost. Oh, my God.
100% record of failure. It was good. It was good to try it, though.
Well, I felt I had to. Did people think it was like Love, Actually?
I think that's what you did. I think some of the adults thought that.
Some of the kids burst into tears because they thought I was the guy from Paddington 2, you know?
The funny thing about this movie is you kind of got into this, Hugh, because it was your wedding day, maybe?
It was an absurd encounter. I was on my way to my wedding with my wife.
I was very late. And trying to cross the road in Sloane Square in London
and a man falls off his bike in front of us, idiot.
So I, oh, Christ, tried to pick him up.
And along comes Guy Ritchie, who I know a bit because I did Man From U.N.C.L.E. with him.
And he said, sorry, Hugh, it's my assistant. He's always falling off his bike. Get up, get up.
So he got up. I said, right, well, anyway, bye. Nice to see you.
Guy said, no, no, wait, wait, wait. I've got a script for you. I said, yes, fine, but I've got to get married.
And I'm really late. He said, no, no, I'm going to tell you. I'm going to pitch you the whole story.
So... So you were late before your wedding because of Guy Ritchie. Yeah, yeah.
And it turned out to be this movie. It's fantastic, by the way.
It's fun, isn't it? No, it's so much fun. It's great.
I mean, you must have gotten to know each other a little bit on set, even though you didn't have...
No. You really haven't. Really, tonight. You really did just meet tonight.
Yeah, pretty much. No, we did a read-through together.
What were your first impressions? We didn't say anything to each other.
But... I tried to talk to him. He didn't really...
He's not one of those people. He's not really talking. A bit grand. Yeah.
What did you think when you were going to work with Matthew McConaughey? What did you think he was going to be like?
I don't like working with very good actors because they show me up.
And I was aware that he'd won bloody Oscars and things like that.
I was not very happy about it. No, yeah. And what did you think about when you're going to work with Hugh Grant?
Well, what you probably don't know about Hugh is how jolly of a character he is.
It's infectious humor and laughter.
And just so happy, it pisses everyone off.
That's not the case at all. This, one of the funniest men I've ever seen.
I'm a miseralist. There's an art to misery.
Matthew, describe your character in The Gentleman. How'd you come up with Mickey?
Mickey, he's an American expat who's living in London
who has a very profitable marijuana empire.
And he's trying to sell off this multi-hundred-million-dollar
empire gracefully and civilly for a fair price.
But because he's in the business with a bunch of scallywags, of course, they're not going to pay the fair price.
So instead of eloquently retiring as I want to, he has to get up and get very violent again
and try to eliminate the bad guys.
Yeah. And, Hugh, your character, I've never seen you play a character like this.
How good is this man? He crushes it.
This is amazing. No, Matthew McConaughey is...
That's very nice. He's a proper actor.
I'm messing around in this film, and I don't know if it comes off, but, yes, I play an incredibly sleazy,
repellent reptile of a human being. He's a private investigator working for a big tabloid newspaper.
And that, of course, was the sort of joke casting on Guy Ritchie's part,
because I've been ranting and raving about tabloid newspapers for years.
But it was quite fun, and I was able to do it. I've now made friends, you see,
during my campaigning with some of the guys who used to hack my phone, steal my medical records,
in one case, burgle my flat. That was weird, because the people I campaign with
give me a party every year, and at each one, they introduce me to another person
who did something terrible to me, and it's just strange.
They say, oh, Hugh, I don't think you've met Bill. He burgled your flat in 1996.
And you go, oh, hi, hi, well, have a drink. I think you know where everything is, you know? I don't know.
-♪♪♪♪ -"Let yourself in. You know the place." You know the place pretty well.
Oh, you know, I had an idea I thought would be kind of fun. I thought it would be kind of fun
if you read a famous line of Matthews, and, Matthew, you read a famous line of Hughes.
Would you be up for that? -"Yes, please." -"Matthew, you can go first."
The first one is Hughes' line from Love, Actually.
-"I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm. Who do you have to screw around here
to get a cup of tea and a chocolate biscuit?"
Wait, but how would, how would... That sounded like him.
But how would you do it as Matthew McConaughey?
Who do you got to screw around here to get a cup of tea and a biscuit?
-"So much hussle." -"That's not bad." And then, Hugh, could you read this line here?
This is from Matthews. -"All right, all right, all right."
Yes! Finally, that's it. That's it again.
